notes.note: L. Cornelius Balbus, born at Gades (Cadiz) in Hispania Ulterior; the first man not of Roman or Italian birth to reach the consulship (40 BC). Granted Roman citizenship by Pompey under the lex Gellia-Cornelia for his service in the Sertorian war (76-72 BC); afterwards an intimate of Caesar, his prefect of engineers (praefectus fabrum) in 61, 59 BC.

Two of the Sertorian battles in eastern Spain (75 BC), in which Pompey's army (with Memmius and Balbus) faced Sertorius and Perperna; Balbus is being claimed as a participant in the major actions of that war.

notes.anchor: Q. Metellus the son of Lucius
notes.note: Q. Caecilius Metellus Numidicus, consul 109 BC, censor 102, victor in Numidia, who chose exile in 100 BC over swearing the oath required by Saturninus's agrarian law: "whose country's safety was sweeter than its sight."

The law of 72 BC carried by the consuls L. Gellius Publicola and Cn. Cornelius Lentulus Clodianus, which authorized Pompey, on the advice of his consilium, to grant Roman citizenship individually for service in the Sertorian war.

A federate or Latin community is made fundus when, by its own vote, it formally accepts a Roman law as binding on its citizens. Cicero's argument is that the prosecutor has confused fundus-status (a free people's voluntary acceptance of Roman civil law) with the question of whether a federate's citizens can become Romans, which has nothing to do with the federate-or-free distinction.

After the lex Julia of 90 BC and the lex Plautia-Papiria of 89 BC offered Roman citizenship to the Italian allies, Heraclea and Naples, both old federate states with privileged treaties, hesitated --- preferring (says Cicero) the freedom of their treaty to citizenship --- and required separate confirmation.

After the rape of the Sabine women, Romulus's reconciliation with the Sabines under T. Tatius incorporated the Sabines into the new Roman polity --- Cicero's foundational example of the Roman practice of taking even enemies into citizenship.

Four Gallic federate peoples whose treaties (Cicero claims) explicitly excepted the right of their citizens to become Romans. By his exceptio est argument, that very exception proves the contrary in the case of Gades: where there is no exception, citizenship transfer is allowed.

L. Marcius, the centurion of the first cohort who took command after the deaths of the Scipio brothers in Spain (211 BC) and is said to have struck the first treaty with Gades; the treaty was renewed by senatorial vote under M. Aemilius Lepidus and Q. Lutatius Catulus, consuls of 78 BC.

The clause maiestatem populi Romani comiter conservanto in the Gaditan treaty (and many federate treaties): "let them comiter (kindly) preserve the majesty of the Roman people." The clause is the formal mark that the federate party is the inferior.

C. Marius, the seven-times consul; the foundational example of the imperator's right to grant citizenship for service. The two cohorts of Camertes were granted citizenship in the field at Aquae Sextiae (102 BC) or Vercellae (101 BC); the M. Annius Appius case is otherwise unattested.

The lex Licinia Mucia of 95 BC (carried by the consuls L. Licinius Crassus and Q. Mucius Scaevola) tightened the rules on usurped citizenship and is widely held to have been one of the proximate causes of the Social War; the Spoletine T. Matrinius case is one of the few attested prosecutions under it.

Cicero's standard formula for Ennius. The Hannibal-fragment hostem qui feriet, mihi Carthaginiensis erit, quisquis erit (Skutsch Annales fr. 234) is one of the most famous fragments of the lost poem. Ennius gives Hannibal the principle Cicero is here arguing for the Roman generals.

notes.anchor: the bronze column behind the rostra
notes.note: The treaty between Rome and the Latins, struck by the consuls Sp. Cassius and Postumus Cominius in 493 BC (the foedus Cassianum), was inscribed on a bronze pillar standing behind the rostra in the Forum; Cicero says he himself remembers seeing it. The column was destroyed in the wake of the Social War.

The Greek-form rites of Ceres at the Aventine temple of Ceres-Liber-Libera (the plebeian triad) were performed by Greek priestesses, naturalized by name --- a senatorial precedent for granting citizenship to a non-citizen for the purpose of public sacrifice. Calliphana of Velia was the most recent case before the Social War.

Balbus, on receiving citizenship, was inscribed in the Tribus Clustumina (one of the older rural tribes); Cicero says the tribe-assignment was a reward to him under the lex Cornelia de ambitu (one of the laws on canvassing) for the prosecution of an unspecified senator.

Theophanes of Mytilene, Pompey's friend and biographer, adopted Balbus --- a form of citizenship-securing arrangement among Greeks. The substantive consequence (says Cicero) was only inheritances from Balbus's own kinsmen.

Praefectus fabrum: the senior staff officer of a Roman general, in charge of the camp's engineering corps but in practice an all-purpose chief-of-staff. Balbus held the post under Caesar in his praetorship (62 BC) and consulship (59 BC).

notes.anchor: those places which by their region define the world
notes.note: Caesar in Gaul; the speech is delivered in the summer of 56 BC, the year of his second campaign across the Rhine and into Britain (the British expedition itself falls in 55-54 BC). The frontier is being pushed out at speed.
